flashcards.Back: A sequential list of permit or deny statements used by a router to filter network traffic based on specific criteria in packet headers.
A type of ACL that filters traffic based solely on the source IPv4 address. it uses the number ranges 1–99 and 1300–1999.
A granular ACL that filters traffic based on source/destination IP, protocol, and port numbers. it uses ranges 100–199 and 2000–2699.
An ACL identified by a descriptive text string instead of a number. This allows for easier management and direct editing of individual lines.
A traditional Cisco ACL identified by a specific numerical ID that determines its type (standard vs extended).
A 32-bit string of bits used in ACLs to specify which parts of an IP address to match. 0 = must match, 1 = ignore (don't care).
The invisible 'deny all' statement at the end of every ACL. if a packet does not match any explicit permit rule, it is automatically dropped.
The processing behavior where a router checks an ACL from top to bottom and stops at the very first entry that matches the packet.
A single line or statement within an ACL that defines a specific filtering rule (e.g., 'permit tcp any any eq 80').
An ACL applied to an interface that filters packets as they arrive at the router, before a routing table lookup is performed.
An ACL applied to an interface that filters packets after the routing decision is made, just before they are sent out of the port.
The interface configuration command used to apply an IPv4 ACL to a specific physical or logical port.
Unique identifiers assigned to each ACE, allowing administrators to insert or delete specific rules without rewriting the entire ACL.
A shortcut used in ACLs to match a single specific IP address, equivalent to using a wildcard mask of 0.0.0.0.
A shortcut used in ACLs to match all possible IP addresses, equivalent to using a wildcard mask of 255.255.255.255.
Standard ACLs should be placed as close to the destination as possible to prevent them from unintentionally blocking traffic to other networks.
Extended ACLs should be placed as close to the source as possible to save bandwidth and CPU resources by dropping unwanted traffic early.
An extended ACL parameter used to permit return TCP traffic from a connection that was initiated from the internal network.
The primary verification command that displays all configured ACLs on the router and the number of packet matches (hits) for each line.
An optional parameter at the end of an ACE that causes the router to generate a syslog message whenever a packet matches that specific rule.
A configuration keyword used to add descriptive comments to an ACL statement to explain its purpose to other administrators.
The 'access-class' command used to apply an ACL to the VTY lines, restricting remote management (Telnet/SSH) access to trusted IPs.
ACLs applied outbound on an interface do not filter packets that are generated by the router itself (e.g., pings from the router CLI).
A router restriction where only one ACL can be applied per interface, per direction (in/out), and per protocol (IPv4/IPv6).
Using extended ACLs to permit or deny specific ICMP message types like echo-requests (pings) or unreachable messages.
An extended ACL feature using the 'range' keyword to match a block of TCP or UDP port numbers in a single statement.
A statistic shown in 'show ip access-lists' that increments every time a packet is matched against a specific permit or deny entry.
A list that is defined but has no ACEs. an empty ACL permits all traffic by default, effectively doing nothing.
The result of subtracting a standard subnet mask from 255.255.255.255 (e.g., 255.255.255.0 becomes 0.0.0.255).
A sub-configuration mode (ip-access-list) that allows for direct line manipulation and descriptive naming of filtering lists.
